docs.title: Home
docs.text:
SkyMapper

Open-source indoor 3D scanning drone Build with off-the-shelf UAV + smartphone payload, and monitor/replay scans in a web viewer.

🚀 Start Here 🧩 Build Hardware 💻 Setup Software 🌐 GitHub

Safety first

SkyMapper involves operating drones indoors. Always follow local laws and safety rules. Keep a safety perimeter and never fly near people.

docs.title: What you will build
docs.text:
• Phone-on-drone payload
  • Print & assemble a lightweight mount

  • Secure smartphone orientation and camera clearance

  • Autonomous scan workflow

  • Start a scan session

  • Drone follows planned views and captures data

  • Web monitoring & replay

  • View trajectory + reconstruction + synchronized video
  • Keep session archives for later

docs.title: Recommended build path
docs.text:
1. Read the “Start Here” page (requirements + first run) → Getting Started
  2. Build the mount and complete pre-flight checks → Hardware
  3. Run the server/viewer, connect phone + controller, and perform your first scan → Software → Operation

docs.title: Project status
docs.text:
• Docs-first: This site prioritizes reproducible build steps and “first success”.
  • Known-good baseline: DJI Phantom 4 Pro V2.0 + iPhone 15 Pro (from our reference build).
  • Help wanted: more tested configurations, mount variants, and troubleshooting cases.

docs.title: Quick links
docs.text:
• Getting Started Requirements, demo replay, quickstart, first scan checklist. Open →

  • Hardware BOM, printing, mount assembly, integration, safety. Open →

  • Software Installation, configuration, viewer, sessions & logs. Open →

docs.title: About SkyMapper
docs.text:
SkyMapper is an open-source project from KIST focused on making autonomous indoor 3D scanning accessible with off-the-shelf drones + smartphones.

This documentation is written for builders and users. The goal is to help you go from zero → first successful scan with minimal guesswork.

docs.title: What SkyMapper is (in plain terms)
docs.text:
• You mount a smartphone on a commercial UAV.
  • The phone provides tracking + RGB-D sensing.
  • A compute node builds a 3D map and plans the next best viewpoints.
  • A web viewer lets you monitor and replay scan sessions.

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
• Reproducible: build-to-spec before customization
  • Low barrier: no custom sensors, no heavy fabrication
  • Docs-first: if it's not in the docs, it doesn't exist (yet)

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text: SkyMapper involves drones. You are responsible for safe operation and compliance with local laws.

docs.title: Contact / Support
docs.text:
• GitHub Issues: bug reports and feature requests
  • Pull Requests: documentation improvements are welcome

docs.title: Citation (optional)
docs.text: If you use SkyMapper in academic work, you may cite our reference paper (see Contribute → License for legal/licensing details).

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: SkyMapper is docs-first: improvements to documentation are valuable.

docs.title: Ways to contribute
docs.text:
• Improve build steps and checklists
  • Add tested configurations (model numbers + photos)
  • Add troubleshooting cases and fixes
  • Improve scripts for reproducible installs

docs.title: Pull request guidelines
docs.text:
• Keep pages short and actionable
  • Prefer checklists and “success criteria”
  • Include screenshots/photos where helpful

docs.title: Documentation Style Guide
docs.text: We aim for: - short sections - checklists - clear success criteria - minimal jargon

docs.title: Page template
docs.text:
1. Goal
  2. Prerequisites
  3. Steps
  4. Success criteria
  5. Troubleshooting links

docs.title: License
docs.text:
Decide and document your licenses clearly.

Common pattern:

  • Software: MIT / Apache-2.0
  • Hardware designs (STL/CAD): CERN-OHL / CC-BY

docs.title: What to do now
docs.text:
1. Choose licenses for software and hardware assets
  2. Add LICENSE files to the repository
  3. State licensing on this page

docs.title: Roadmap
docs.text: This page is intentionally practical.

docs.title: Docs roadmap (immediate)
docs.text:
• Add “known-good builds” matrix
  • Add QR marker download + print guide
  • Add a real demo session artifact for replay
  • Add a one-command Docker quickstart

docs.title: Technical roadmap (future)
docs.text:
• More robust drift handling
  • Better mapping performance
  • More tested drone/phone compatibility

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
Welcome to SkyMapper.

Goal: help you reach first successful scan quickly and safely.

docs.title: Choose your path
docs.text:
• Path A — Demo first (no drone)
  • See what outputs look like (video + reconstruction snapshots)

  • Understand the workflow before building Go to Demo Replay →

  • Path B — Hardware first

  • Build the mount and verify flight stability Go to Hardware Overview →

  • Path C — Full system

  • Requirements → software setup → first scan checklist Go to Quickstart (Full System) →

docs.title: What “success” looks like
docs.text:
• You can open the web viewer.
  • The phone is streaming.
  • You can start a scan session.
  • A session archive is saved and replayable.

docs.title: Next steps
docs.text:
1. Read Requirements (hardware + network + safety)
  2. Run Demo Replay or proceed to Quickstart

docs.title: Demo Replay (No Drone)
docs.text:
This page is a zero-hardware preview of what SkyMapper produces.

You can use it to: - understand the workflow - see expected outputs - verify that your browser can play the demo video

docs.title: Demo video
docs.text: Recorded scan session (reference)

docs.title: Output preview
docs.text:
• Real environment

  • Reconstruction in viewer

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: When you’re ready, continue to: - Requirements → - Quickstart (Full System) →

docs.title: First Scan Checklist
docs.text: Use this page as a pre-flight and success checklist.

docs.title: Safety
docs.text:
• No people in the scan area
  • Emergency landing/manual takeover tested
  • Prop guards installed (recommended)
  • Battery check (drone + phone + controller)

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
• Mount is rigid (no rattle)
  • Phone cannot slide during acceleration
  • Camera view is unobstructed
  • Weight distribution is acceptable (hover test)

docs.title: Network
docs.text:
• All devices on the same Wi‑Fi SSID
  • Server IP is reachable from phone/tablet
  • Firewall allows required ports

docs.title: Marker
docs.text:
• QR-code marker placed flat, visible, well-lit
  • Phone app successfully initializes coordinate frame

docs.title: Launch sequence (recommended)
docs.text:
1. Start server + viewer
  2. Connect phone stream (confirm updates)
  3. Connect controller (confirm manual works)
  4. Take off and hover (stability check)
  5. Start autonomous scan session
  6. Monitor in viewer; intervene if needed
  7. End mission → confirm session is saved
  8. Replay session in viewer

docs.title: Success criteria
docs.text:
• Viewer shows live updates during flight
  • Session directory/bundle is created at mission end
  • Replay shows the scan trajectory and outputs

docs.title: After first success
docs.text:
• Try a larger space
  • Improve marker placement and lighting
  • Start documenting your own tested configuration (model numbers + photos)

docs.title: Quickstart (Full System)
docs.text:
This guide is written for first success, not for perfect customization.

Because SkyMapper has multiple components, the recommended order is:

1) Bring up the server + viewer 2) Connect the phone and confirm streaming 3) Enable autonomous control (only after streaming is stable)

docs.title: 1) Clone the repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/<YOUR_ORG>/<YOUR_REPO>.git
cd <YOUR_REPO>

docs.title: 2) Choose an install method
docs.text:
Option A — Docker (recommended)Option B — Native install

Docker provides the most reproducible setup across machines.

docker compose up --build

Use native install if you plan to develop/hack the code.

python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

This page is template-first

Replace the commands above with your repo’s actual scripts once finalized (e.g., ./scripts/run_server.sh, roslaunch ..., etc.).

docs.title: 3) Start the viewer
docs.text:
Open your viewer URL in a browser:

  • http://<SERVER_IP>:<PORT>/

You should see: - a blank scene / UI - connection status panel - no console errors

docs.title: 4) Connect the phone
docs.text:
On the phone: - start the mobile app - set the server address - confirm tracking is started - confirm the stream is connected

On the server: - verify that pose/frame messages are arriving - confirm the viewer updates (trajectory/status)

docs.title: 5) Enable autonomous control (tablet/controller)
docs.text:
Only after streaming is stable:

  • connect the controller device
  • verify manual mode works
  • switch to autonomous mode
  • test with a small/safe mission first

docs.title: 6) End mission and replay
docs.text:
At the end of a mission, SkyMapper should save a session artifact: - reconstruction data - time-stamped trajectory - synchronized video/logs

Replay it in the viewer.

docs.title: If something fails
docs.text: Go to: - Troubleshooting → - First Scan Checklist →

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
This page defines the baseline configuration that the docs are written for.

Known-good baseline (reference build)

  • Drone: DJI Phantom 4 Pro V2.0
  • Phone: iPhone 15 Pro
  • Mount: simple 3D-printed adapter
  • Control UI: tablet interface (for mission control)

You can use different hardware, but start with a known-good baseline if possible.

docs.title: UAV
docs.text:
Minimum: - A commercial UAV that supports programmatic control (SDK / waypoint missions) - Sufficient payload for phone + mount (~200–250 g is a common starting point)

Recommended: - Prop guards for indoor operation - Stable hover performance

docs.title: Phone
docs.text:
Minimum: - AR-capable phone with stable VIO - Depth sensing (solid-state LiDAR or high-quality depth via stereo/ToF) is recommended

Recommended: - iPhone Pro models with ARKit depth

docs.title: Compute node (server)
docs.text:
Minimum: - Modern laptop/desktop (Linux or macOS), or mini-PC - Reliable network interface (prefer wired LAN to the router)

Recommended: - Dedicated machine for mapping/planning to reduce jitter

docs.title: 3D printer
docs.text:
Minimum: - FDM printer with decent dimensional accuracy

Recommended: - PETG or ABS for mounts (PLA can deform with heat)

docs.title: Network
docs.text:
Most “it doesn’t connect” problems are network issues.

  • All devices must be on the same Wi‑Fi SSID (unless you explicitly route traffic)
  • Prefer a dedicated router/AP for demos
  • Avoid captive portals (public Wi‑Fi)

docs.title: Workspace prep (indoor scan area)
docs.text:
• Remove small obstacles and reflective clutter
  • Ensure adequate lighting and visible texture (blank white walls are harder)
  • Mark a safe takeoff/landing zone

docs.title: Fiducial marker (coordinate initialization)
docs.text:
SkyMapper uses a QR-code fiducial marker in the scan area to initialize the phone’s coordinate frame.

Practical tips: - Print on matte paper - Place flat and well-lit - Avoid glare and extreme viewing angles

(If you provide the marker image in the repo, link it here.)

docs.title: Safety checklist (minimum)
docs.text:
• You have a spotter (recommended)
  • No people in the flight area
  • Emergency landing / manual takeover tested
  • Props/guards installed and verified

docs.title: Hardware Overview
docs.text:
Hardware for SkyMapper is designed to be: - reproducible - lightweight - buildable with a basic FDM printer

If this is your first build, follow build-to-spec before customizing.

  • BOM Required parts list and suggested options. Open →

  • Printing Materials, settings, and print orientation notes. Open →

  • Mount Assembly Step-by-step assembly with checklists. Open →

  • Drone Integration Balance, camera clearance, and pre-flight checks. Open →

docs.title: Bill of Materials (BOM)
docs.text:
Start conservative

Begin with a known-good baseline configuration and only then swap parts.

docs.title: Core components
docs.text:
• Drone (SDK-capable)
  • Smartphone (AR-capable)
  • Mount (3D printed)
  • Fasteners / straps (secure phone)
  • Optional damping pads

docs.title: Reference build (tested baseline)
docs.text:
• Drone: DJI Phantom 4 Pro V2.0
  • Phone: iPhone 15 Pro
  • Mount: 3D-printed adapter + straps

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Hex keys / screwdrivers
  • Calipers (optional but helpful)
  • Threadlocker (low strength, optional)

docs.title: Nice-to-have
docs.text:
• Prop guards
  • Spare props
  • Dedicated Wi‑Fi router/AP

docs.title: Drone Integration
docs.text: This page covers balance, clearance, and pre-flight checks after mounting.

docs.title: Integration reference
docs.text: Example integration (reference)

docs.title: Balance & stability
docs.text:
• Perform a hover test in a safe area
  • If drift/instability increases:
  • re-check center of gravity
  • reduce payload
  • stiffen the mount (remove flex)

docs.title: Camera clearance
docs.text:
• Ensure props/landing gear do not dominate the phone’s view
  • Avoid occluding the phone’s depth sensor area

docs.title: Pre-flight checklist (hardware)
docs.text:
• Mount rigid (no rattle)
  • Phone locked (cannot slide)
  • Camera view clear
  • All straps secure
  • Emergency stop/manual takeover ready

docs.title: Mount Assembly
docs.text: Use this page as your step-by-step build checklist.

docs.title: Mount reference images
docs.text: Mount structure Phone holder detail

docs.title: Assembly steps
docs.text:
1. Dry-fit
  2. Phone inserts smoothly

  3. No force required

  4. Install fasteners / inserts (if used)

  5. Do not overtighten into plastic

  6. Add straps/velcro

  7. Straps should not cover camera/IMU areas

  8. Vibration damping (optional)

  9. Add pads only where they do not allow “wobble”

docs.title: Mount QA checklist
docs.text:
• Phone is centered and stable
  • Camera view is clear
  • No contact with moving parts
  • No sharp edges / abrasion points

docs.title: Printing
docs.text: This page covers print settings and practical tips for mount parts.

docs.title: Material
docs.text:
Recommended: - PETG (good balance of stiffness and durability) - ABS/ASA (heat resistant, requires enclosure)

Avoid (for long-term mounts): - PLA (can deform under heat / sunlight)

docs.title: Baseline settings (start here)
docs.text:
• Layer height: 0.2 mm
  • Perimeters: 4+
  • Infill: 30–40%
  • Supports: only where needed
  • Heatset inserts: optional

docs.title: Fit check
docs.text: Before final assembly: - dry-fit the phone - verify no sharp edges touch phone body - verify strap channels are clean

docs.title: Print orientation tips
docs.text:
• Align layer lines to resist strap tension
  • Avoid thin cantilevers without supports
  • If the mount vibrates, increase wall thickness and add ribs

docs.title: Safety
docs.text: Indoor flight is risky. This page is intentionally short and strict.

docs.title: Do not fly if…
docs.text:
• there are people in the scan area
  • you cannot immediately take manual control
  • your mount is loose or flexible
  • lighting is poor and tracking is unstable

docs.title: Recommended safety setup
docs.text:
• Prop guards (highly recommended)
  • Safety perimeter (cones/tape)
  • Spotter (recommended)
  • Clear takeoff/landing zone

docs.title: Emergency procedures
docs.text:
• Practice manual takeover before autonomous mode
  • Practice landing with low battery
  • Know the kill switch / emergency landing sequence

docs.title: Data Export
docs.text:
SkyMapper outputs are useful for downstream tasks:

  • facility documentation
  • inspection annotations
  • ML perception pipelines (e.g., defect detection)

docs.title: Export checklist
docs.text:
• session folder is complete
  • metadata included (device models, versions)
  • coordinate frame definition documented

docs.title: Recommended export formats
docs.text:
• Trajectory: CSV
  • Video: MP4
  • Reconstruction: mesh/voxel output (document exact format in Reference → File Formats)

docs.title: Live monitoring
docs.text: During a mission, confirm: - viewer status is “connected” - trajectory updates - reconstruction updates

docs.title: Replay
docs.text: After mission: - open the session list - select the latest session - scrub through time (trajectory/video)

docs.title: Sharing results
docs.text:
• export session folder
  • include metadata and logs if sharing for debugging

docs.title: Running a Scan
docs.text: This page describes the recommended flight workflow.

docs.title: Workflow (4 steps)
docs.text:
1. Place the QR-code fiducial marker
  2. Initialize coordinate frame in the phone app
  3. Take off and start autonomous scanning from the controller
  4. Monitor in viewer; end mission; replay the session

docs.title: During flight
docs.text:
• Always keep manual takeover ready
  • Pause/land if tracking becomes unstable
  • Watch battery threshold behavior

docs.title: End mission
docs.text:
• Confirm session is saved
  • Replay in viewer and verify outputs

docs.title: System Architecture
docs.text: This page is a reference for how components connect.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
• Phone (tracking + RGB-D)
  • Compute node (mapping/planning)
  • Controller (mission control)
  • Web viewer (monitoring/replay)

docs.title: Data flows (high level)
docs.text:
• Phone → compute node: pose + RGB-D stream
  • Compute node → controller: goals/waypoints
  • Compute node → web server: map/telemetry

docs.title: File Formats
docs.text: Document your actual session outputs here.

docs.title: Session metadata (meta.json)
docs.text: Suggested fields: - timestamp - drone model - phone model - software versions - network info - mission duration

docs.title: Trajectory
docs.text: Suggested: - CSV with t, x, y, z, qx, qy, qz, qw

docs.title: Reconstruction output
docs.text: Document: - coordinate frame - units (meters) - mesh format (PLY/OBJ) - voxel/TSDF export format (if applicable)

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
AR/VIO: Visual-Inertial Odometry (camera + IMU tracking)

RGB-D: color + depth stream

Session: a bundle of outputs saved for replay

Viewer: the web UI for monitoring/replay

Controller: ground station interface to start/stop missions and override safety

docs.title: Network Ports
docs.text:
Document your actual ports here.

Component Protocol Port Notes Viewer HTTP/WS 8080 UI + control Streaming WebRTC TBD pose + RGB-D Telemetry WS TBD status/health

Avoid magic numbers

Keep ports in a single config file and reference it here.

docs.title: Software Overview
docs.text:
SkyMapper software consists of multiple components that work together:

  • Mobile app (phone): tracking + RGB-D capture
  • Compute node (server): mapping + planning + archiving
  • Controller (tablet): mission control and safety override
  • Web viewer: monitoring + replay

  • Installation How to install server/viewer and dependencies. Open →

  • Configuration Where to set IP/ports, data paths, and runtime options. Open →

  • Mobile App Connect phone and verify streaming. Open →

  • Sessions & Logs What is saved, where it is saved, and what logs to collect. Open →

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
SkyMapper usually needs configuration in three places:

1) Server / viewer 2) Mobile app 3) Controller

docs.title: Minimal configuration checklist
docs.text:
• Server IP address
  • Viewer URL/port
  • Data/session output directory
  • Streaming settings (fps/bitrate)
  • Safety settings (battery threshold, return-to-home)

docs.title: Suggested config table (fill in)
docs.text:
Setting Where Example SERVER_IP phone/controller 192.168.0.10 VIEWER_PORT server 8080 STREAM_FPS phone 30 VIDEO_BITRATE phone 4M SESSION_DIR server ./sessions/

One source of truth

Prefer a single .env or config.yaml shared by all components.

docs.title: Controller (Tablet / Ground Station)
docs.text:
The controller is responsible for:

  • mission start/stop
  • mode switching (manual/autonomous)
  • safety override (pause/emergency)

docs.title: Bring-up order
docs.text:
1. Confirm server + viewer works
  2. Confirm phone streaming works
  3. Only then start controller and test manual mode
  4. Switch to autonomous mode with a small mission

docs.title: Safety expectations
docs.text:
• Manual takeover must always be possible
  • Provide a clear “pause” and “land now” action
  • Battery threshold should trigger automatic return/landing

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
This page describes installation for the compute node (server + viewer).

Docker (recommended)Native 
git clone https://github.com/wrlab/SkyMapper.git
cd <YOUR_REPO>
docker compose up --build

git clone https://github.com/wrlab/SkyMapper.git
cd SkyMapper
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt

Replace template commands

Update this page with your actual scripts and required versions once finalized.

docs.title: Mobile App
docs.text: This page covers phone-side setup and how to verify streaming.

docs.title: Goals
docs.text:
You should be able to confirm:

  • tracking is started
  • the QR-code marker initializes the coordinate frame
  • the server receives pose/frames
  • the viewer updates

docs.title: Connect to the server
docs.text:
1. Set the server IP/port in the app
  2. Start streaming
  3. Confirm the viewer receives updates

docs.title: Common failure points
docs.text:
• phone and server are not on the same SSID
  • wrong IP/port (use the server’s LAN IP)
  • firewall blocks ports
  • low light / low texture causing tracking instability

docs.title: Sessions & Logs
docs.text: This page documents what SkyMapper saves and what to attach to bug reports.

docs.title: Session artifact (recommended)
docs.text:
At mission end, save:

  • reconstruction output
  • time-stamped trajectory
  • synchronized video (optional)
  • runtime metadata (versions, device models)
  • logs

docs.title: Directory structure (suggested)
docs.text:
sessions/
  2026-03-12_14-05-33/
    meta.json
    trajectory.csv
    reconstruction/
    video.mp4
    logs/

docs.title: Bug report checklist
docs.text: Attach: - server logs - controller logs - phone app logs (if available) - a screenshot of the viewer - your configuration values (IP/ports)

docs.title: Web Viewer
docs.text:
The viewer is your main UX surface:

  • monitor a scan in real-time
  • replay a session archive
  • inspect areas of interest

docs.title: What you should see
docs.text:
During a mission: - growing reconstruction/map - UAV trajectory - synchronized video (if enabled)

After a mission: - a session you can replay

docs.title: Browser notes
docs.text:
• Use a modern Chromium-based browser for best results
  • If video doesn’t play, confirm codec support and HTTPS requirements (if applicable)

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Start here if something isn’t working.

  • Hardware issues Mount vibrations, instability, camera occlusion. Open →

  • Network issues Connection failures, choppy streams, wrong IP/ports. Open →

  • Software issues Server crashes, viewer errors, session not saved. Open →

docs.title: Before asking for help
docs.text: Collect: - logs (server/controller) - screenshots of viewer - device models and OS versions

docs.title: Drone unstable
docs.text:
• verify weight distribution
  • stiffen mount
  • reduce payload

docs.title: Video blur / vibration
docs.text:
• add damping pads (without allowing wobble)
  • increase mount stiffness (walls/ribs)
  • reduce resonance points

docs.title: Phone view occluded
docs.text:
• adjust mount position/angle
  • ensure depth sensor area is unobstructed

docs.title: Viewer loads but no data
docs.text:
• verify phone is streaming
  • verify correct server IP/port
  • ensure same Wi‑Fi SSID

docs.title: Choppy streaming
docs.text:
• reduce fps/bitrate
  • use wired LAN for server
  • use a dedicated router/AP

docs.title: Random disconnects
docs.text:
• check Wi‑Fi interference
  • disable power saving on devices
  • confirm firewall rules

docs.title: Server won’t start
docs.text:
• check dependency versions
  • run in verbose mode and inspect logs

docs.title: Viewer errors
docs.text:
• open browser console
  • confirm viewer URL/ports
  • try a different browser

docs.title: Session not saved
docs.text:
• verify output directory permissions
  • confirm disk space
  • confirm end-of-mission hook is triggered
